# Choosing the Right Approach for Animal Health Market Research

![](https://vetaccessqa.com/hubfs/1-Jul-09-2025-02-16-15-9249-PM.png)

## Choosing the Right Approach for Animal Health Market Research

Are you bringing a new animal health product to the market or testing a message that needs to resonate with both veterinarians and pet owners? The success of your strategy depends on asking the right questions and choosing the right research approach to get the answers.

This space comes with unique considerations to take into account. Your audience could include veterinary professionals, livestock producers, or pet owners, all with different experiences, knowledge levels, and decision-making processes. Other valuable audience groups, like large-animal vets or farmers, can be difficult to reach during busy times of year such as calving or harvest season. Factoring in both audience dynamics and timing is key to collecting meaningful insights.

Choosing the right research method starts with understanding the difference between qualitative and quantitative approaches. Qualitative research explores ideas, motivations, and experiences whereas quantitative research focuses on gathering measurable data and identifying trends and behaviors.

Both forms of research can offer valuable insights, but selecting the right approach depends on what your specific goals are.

## Understanding Your Research Objectives

Before choosing a research approach, it is essential to  [define what you are trying to learn](/blog/why-is-market-research-important) and who you are trying to hear from. These objectives will shape the entire project from methodology to participant selection.

Start by asking: what questions are we trying to answer? For example, are you looking into market size, testing new messaging, or identifying unmet needs? Each objective calls for a different research strategy.

Next, consider your target audience. Are you looking to hear from veterinarians, livestock producers, vet techs, or pet owners? These groups each bring a unique perspective and their feedback can be highly influential.

Finally, be clear about whether you’re in an exploratory phase or looking to validate existing ideas. Exploratory research often leans on qualitative methods to uncover insights while validation typically requires quantitative data to confirm assumptions or measure impact.

## Quantitative Research: When and Why to Use It

Quantitative research focuses on gathering measurable, numerical data from surveys, tracking studies, or segmentation to help answer key business questions. This approach is ideal for when you need clear, statistically reliable results to support decision making.

Quantitative research is best suited for:

The major advantage of quantitative research is its ability to provide statistically robust insights across a broad audience. It is also useful for identifying trends, tracking changes over time, and validating assumptions with confidence.

That said, this approach requires careful design. Success depends on asking the right questions and reaching the right participants. Poorly worded surveys or misaligned targeting can provide misleading results.

## Qualitative Research: When and Why to Use It

Qualitative research is all about exploring ideas, motivations, and experiences in depth. This approach uses methods like in-depth interviews, focus groups, or ethnographic observation to uncover the “why” behind attitudes and behaviors.

Qualitative research is ideal for:

The strength of qualitative research lies in its ability to generate rich, detailed insights that numbers alone can’t provide. It is especially valuable for idea generation, identifying unmet needs, or shaping early-stage strategies.

However, qualitative studies typically involve smaller sample size which makes it more of a challenge to generalize to a larger population. They’re not designed to produce statistically representative results, but they can offer the deeper context needed to guide your next steps.

## Mixed Methods: Getting the Best of Both Worlds

Sometimes the best approach combines qualitative and quantitative research to leverage the strengths of both. Mixed methods allows you to explore ideas deeply and then validate them at scale providing a more complete understanding of the market.

In the animal health sector, this often means starting with a qualitative discovery to uncover key insights, followed by quantitative surveys to confirm findings across a larger audience. For example, conducting in-depth interviews with veterinarians or pet owners can help refine survey questions to ensure they resonate with your target group.

Another common hybrid approach is using pre-survey interviews or focus groups to improve question framing, which helps avoid misunderstandings and increases data quality.

By blending methods, you gain rich, actionable insights while also securing statistically sound results to guide confident decision making.

## Methodology Pitfalls to Avoid

Choosing the right research method is crucial, but it is equally important to avoid common pitfalls that can undermine your results.

Being mindful of these pitfalls helps ensure your research is well-targeted, relevant, and actionable.

## Vet Access: Your Roadmap to Effective Marketing Insights

At Vet Access, we understand the unique challenges of conducting market research in the animal health industry. Our deep knowledge of veterinary professionals, livestock producers, and pet owners allows us to tailor methodologies specifically to your goals. Whether you need qualitative insights or robust quantitative data, our team designs custom research solutions that deliver clear, actionable results.

We have proven expertise in accessing hard-to-reach respondents such as DVMs, livestock producers, and veterinary support staff to ensure your research captures the [voices that matter most](/case-study-every-voice-matters-engaging-the-full-spectrum-of-veterinary-professionals). Partnering with Vet Access means working with experts who know how to match methodology to objectives and unlock meaningful market insights.

Choosing the right research approach is essential to uncovering credible, actionable insights in the animal health market. Whether you’re exploring new ideas or validating strategies, aligning your methodology with your objectives ensures your research delivers real value.

If you’re ready to design a market research plan tailored to your specific goals and audience, Vet Access can help. Use our free [DIY Research Plan tool](/diy-research-plan) to get started building your strategy today.

# Qualitative Research in Veterinary Medical Education: Part 1-Principles of Qualitative Design

# Qualitative Research in Veterinary Medical Education: Part 1-Principles of Qualitative Design

### Authors

## Abstract

Qualitative methodologies are relative newcomers to health sciences education research. While they may look very different to their quantitative counterparts in terms of size and scope, when well-applied they offer a fresh perspective and generate valuable research findings. Although qualitative research is being increasingly conducted in veterinary medical education, there are few contextualized resources to assist those who would like to develop their expertise in this area. In this article, we address this by introducing the principles of qualitative research design in a veterinary medical education context. Drawing from a range of contemporary resources, we explore the types of research goals and questions that are amenable to qualitative inquiry and discuss the process of formulating a worthwhile research question. We explain what research paradigms are and introduce readers to some of the methodological options available to them in qualitative research. Examples from veterinary medical education are used to illustrate key points. In a second companion article, we will focus on the decisions that need to be made regarding data sampling, collection, and analysis. We will also consider how qualitative research is evaluated, and discuss how qualitative findings are applied. Taken together, the two articles build an understanding of qualitative research, illuminate its potential to contribute to the scholarship of teaching and learning in veterinary medical education, and equip readers with an improved capacity to appraise its value.

**Keywords:**
qualitative research veterinary education; veterinary medical education research; veterinary qualitative methodology; veterinary qualitative research criteria; veterinary qualitative research design.

METHODS article

Front. Vet. Sci., 18 February 2020

Sec. Veterinary Epidemiology and Economics

Volume 7 - 2020 | [https://doi.org/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070](https://doi.org/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070)

This article is part of the Research Topic Principles and Challenges of Fundamental Methods in Veterinary Epidemiology and Economics[View all 16 articles](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/9562/principles-and-challenges-of-fundamental-methods-in-veterinary-epidemiology-and-economics/magazine)

Qualitative Research for One Health: From Methodological Principles to Impactful Applications
=============================================================================================

[![Image 5: Chris Degeling ](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/716401/74)Chris Degeling](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/716401)1*[![Image 6: Melanie Rock](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/426708/74)Melanie Rock](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/426708)2

*   1 Faculty of Social Science, Australian Centre for Health Engagement, Evidence and Values, School of Health and Society, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW, Australia
*   2 Department of Community Health Sciences, Cumming School of Medicine, University of Calgary, Calgary, AB, Canada

The One Health concept has inspired a rich vein of applied research and scholarly reflection over the past decade, yet with little influence from qualitative methodologists. With this overview, we describe the underpinning assumptions, purposes, and potential pitfalls of data collection techniques and methods of data analysis in key qualitative research methodologies. Our aim is to enhance One Health collaborations involving qualitative researchers, veterinary epidemiologists, and veterinary economists. There exist several distinct traditions of qualitative research, from which we draw selectively for illustrative purposes. Notwithstanding important distinctions, we emphasize commonalities and the potential for collaborative impact. The most important commonality is a shared focus on contextualizing human behavior and experience–culturally, economically, historically, and socially. We demonstrate that in-depth attention to context can assist veterinary economists and epidemiologists in drawing lessons from the implementation of policies and programs. In other words, qualitative researchers can assist One Health teams in distilling insights from “success stories,” but also from adverse events and unintended consequences. As a result, qualitative researchers can contribute to One Health research and policy discussions by formulating more accurate and contextually-relevant parameters for future quantitative studies. When performed well, qualitative methodologies can help veterinary economists and epidemiologists to develop impactful research questions, to create more accurate and contextually-relevant parameters for quantitative studies, and to develop policy recommendations and interventions that are attuned to the political and socio-cultural context of their implementation. In sketching out the properties and features of influential methodologies, we underscore the value of working with seasoned qualitative researchers to incorporate questions about “what,” “how,” and “why” in mixed-methods research designs.

Background
----------

Over the past decade, the One Health concept has gained traction in policy, practice, and research. Thus far, economics has proved to be the social science with the most influence in One Health research and related applications ([1](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B1)). Economic evaluations certainly can complement epidemiological models in One Health research. Even so, social sciences besides economics could also play important roles. Here we are thinking about disciplines such as anthropology, geography, philosophy, psychology, and sociology ([2](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B2)). Within these social sciences, qualitative research has deep roots; and within professional fields that focus on human health, such as medicine, nursing, and public health, qualitative research has become increasingly integrated ([3](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B3), [4](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B4)). This article, therefore, seeks to elevate the place of qualitative research in One Health scholarship and applications.

The goal of qualitative research is the development of concepts which help us to understand human behavior and social phenomena in their natural settings, giving emphasis to the meanings, experiences, and views of those implicated and effected ([3](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B3), [5](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B5)). The conditions and systems that produce health in individuals and their populations have long been a focus of qualitative researchers. Public health problems (including those involving other species) are increasingly derived from interactions between human behavior and social and technical systems. Veterinary public health researchers, epidemiologists and policymakers can benefit from integrating insights from qualitative research into their understanding of animal health, not least to improve policies and interventions for the management of the incidence and risks of disease in animals and the provision of related services ([6](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B6), [7](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B7)). Disciplines such as sociology, geography and anthropology have made significant contribution to our capacity to understand public health problems, and they solutions that are both novel and contextually-appropriate.

We have written this article with veterinary economists and epidemiologists in mind, especially those who may wish to collaborate with qualitative researchers. Acknowledging that other disciplines relevant to One Health such as public health and the environmental sciences could benefit from greater engagement with social science methodologies, for the purposes of this special journal issue we wish to assist veterinary economists and epidemiologists with studies that combine quantitative with qualitative methodologies. Our overall aim, therefore, is to impart foundational knowledge regarding qualitative methodologies and applications. The references that we cite, in addition, could provide additional guidance for readers who wish to delve more deeply into qualitative methodologies and their potential for research regarding One Health. To begin, we call attention to the importance of interpretation and meaning in qualitative research. This emphasis leads us to compare two popular approaches, namely **framework analysis** and **grounded theory**. After outlining some of the key features of each, we distinguish methodologies from methods. Then we describe some popular methods for collecting and creating qualitative data; these include interviews, storytelling, and texts. We provide examples in which such data have been employed, synthesized and interpreted to enhance veterinary findings, so that they are better attuned to their political and socio-cultural context.

Considerable expertise and skill are required for impactful research that combines qualitative with quantitative methodologies. Integrating qualitative studies into larger mixed-methods requires robust research design and processes. Experience with and expertise in the method used is a must; insufficient expertise and practical experience can lead to a superficial and even harmful impacts. Because analysis begins at the same time as data collection in most qualitative social science research, in this article we have foregrounded a descriptive summary of different types of data analysis over the standard toolbox of qualitative data collection techniques found in methods textbook. Our purpose is to highlight that robust research processes require that the technique used for analysis should be determined as part of the initial research design—and not chosen as an afterthought once data collection is underway. Of note, researchers should design their studies from the outset with an approach to analysis in mind. By implication, veterinary economists and epidemiologists should include qualitative researchers from the outset if they intend to undertake a qualitative investigation as part of a larger multi-method study. Moreover, even as we invite our colleagues in veterinary economics and epidemiology to consider including qualitative researchers on their teams, we caution against superficiality. In our experience, qualitative researchers are best positioned to contribute when One Health projects have been designed collaboratively, with them and with communities.

Foundations and shared Assumptions in Qualitative Research Methodologies
------------------------------------------------------------------------

While both qualitative and quantitative research can be used to investigate similar topics of relevance to veterinary economics and epidemiology, each will address a different type of question. In relation to the use of an animal vaccine, for example, a quantitative study could determine the proportion and demographic characteristics of owners who give the vaccine to their animals over a set period of time. Similarly, questionnaires that try and capture people's intentions can test the relative importance of different factors thought to be important to their decisions through the statistical analysis of responses to a series of standardized closed questions [See ([8](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/10.3389/fvets.2020.00070/full#B8), [9](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/veterinary-science/articles/1

enriched_points.expanded_content: This research utilizes both qualitative and quantitative methods to capture diverse data, enabling a comprehensive understanding of veterinary public health issues and stakeholder perspectives.
A combination of surveys, interviews, and observational methods will be employed to gather rich data, ensuring a robust framework for analyzing veterinary public health trends.

Interviews with key stakeholders, including veterinarians and policymakers, will provide qualitative insights, helping to inform effective public health policies and interventions based on real-world experiences.
